data.title: Asian markets trade lower on the last day of the week
data.description:
Asian markets are trading lower despite Wall Street closing higher on Thursday after President Donald Trump delayed tariffs on critical minerals and said he does not plan to remove Fed Chair Jerome Powell.

Japan (NKY:IND) -0.05%. December producer prices rose 2.4% from a year earlier, matching consensus expectations and easing from a 2.7% increase in the prior period.

China (SHCOMP) fell 0.22%, extending losses for the fourth day. Metals fell after China moved to curb high-frequency trading following frenzied futures activity that had fueled global price gains. Regulators ordered exchanges including the Shanghai Futures Exchange to remove HFT servers from data centers. Copper, zinc and aluminum declined in Shanghai and London after earlier record rallies. 

Hong Kong (HSI) fell 0.27%.

Australia (AS51) gained 0.39%.

U.S. stock futures edged higher on Friday, extending the previous session’s gains, with Dow futures up 0.60%, S&P 500 futures rising 0.26%, and Nasdaq futures gaining 0.25%.

data.title: Boeing secures new labor agreement with Wichita union
data.description:
Boeing (BA) has struck a provisional labor deal with SPEEA, the union representing 1,600 technical employees who became part of the company following its acquisition of Spirit AeroSystems Holdings last year.

The contract offer includes a $6,000 ratification bonus, a 20% increase to wage pools over 58 months, SPEEA said in a statement Thursday.

“This proposal hits most of the areas we tried to address,” said Wes Gardner, the negotiation team’s vice chairman, who also cited improved pay, time off, benefits, and bonuses—and improved contract language regarding retention ratings, flex time, and working from home.

“This is a huge increase for our SPEEA family,” Gardner said.

SPEEA’s WTPU represents aerospace professionals in non-engineering roles at Boeing’s recently reacquired Wichita operations. Their six-year contract expires Jan. 31, with a new agreement set to take effect Feb. 1.

Union members will have until 5 p.m. (CT) Jan. 30 to review the offer and vote on it.

data.title: Friday's Economic Calendar
data.description:
9:15 AM Industrial Production

The Federal Reserve's monthly index of industrial production and the related capacity indexes and capacity utilization rates cover manufacturing, mining, and electric and gas utilities. 

A muted 0.1 percent rise in output is the call for December, with capacity at 76.0 percent, flat from November.

10:00 AM Housing Market Index

The housing market index is a monthly composite that tracks home builder assessments of present and future sales as well as buyer traffic.

The index is expected to tick up again to 40 in January from 39 in December as builder sentiment gradually recovers from last summer’s lows.

10:50 AM Susan Collins Speaks

Boston Federal Reserve Bank President Susan Collins gives opening and introductory remarks before the Outlook '26 New England Economic Forum.

11:00 AM Michelle Bowman Speaks

Federal Reserve Vice Chair for Supervision Michelle Bowman speaks on the outlook for the economy and monetary policy before the Outlook '26 New England Economic Forum.

3:30 PM Philip Jefferson Speaks

Federal Reserve Vice Chair Philip Jefferson speaks on the economic outlook and monetary policy implementation before the American Institute for Economic Research, Shadow Open Market Committee, and Florida Atlantic University Conference.

data.title: Micron director buys $7.8M in common stock
data.description:
• Micron (MU) disclosed that its director Teyin Liu bought 23.2K shares of common stock in a total transaction size of $7.8M.
  • The transaction was made on January 13-14, 2026. Following these transactions, Liu directly owns 25,910 shares of Micron.
  • Micron (MU) shares gained 1% in extended trading on Thursday.

data.title: Chemours to sell former titanium dioxide site in Taiwan
data.description:
• The Chemours Company (CC) has signed a set of definitive agreements to sell the remaining land at its former titanium dioxide manufacturing location in Kuan Yin, Taiwan, it said on Thursday. 
  • The land sale will generate approximately $360 million in gross cash proceeds, and the deal is expected to substantially close by mid-year 2026.
  • The company will apply cash proceeds to reduce its debt obligations. 
  • Chemours (CC) shares rose 2.4% in extended trading on Thursday.

data.title: J.B. Hunt targets $100M+ cost savings momentum through 2026 as freight market remains fragile
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: J.B. Hunt Transport Services, Inc. (JBHT) Q4 2025

Management View

  • Shelley Simpson, President and CEO, stated the company "began 2025 with clear expectations, but when the external environment shifted, we responded by adapting our strategy." She highlighted that operational excellence and safety performance remain core strengths: "Our service levels and safety performance remain exceptional, and they are key differentiators for us in the industry." Simpson emphasized progress in scaling investments and margin repair, adding, "While we made meaningful progress in both areas, I recognize there is still more work ahead."
  • Simpson addressed rail consolidation: "We remain rooted in our commitment to our customers and providing excellent intermodal service and also to our shareholders to create lasting long-term value. We continue to have conversations with all Class I railroads and those conversations are progressing."
  • Discussing 2026, Simpson outlined three key priorities: "First, we're focused on disciplined growth through operational excellence... Second, we will leverage our investments in our people, technology and capacity into clear and sustainable competitive advantages... Third, we will continue to repair our margins to drive long-term value for our shareholders."
  • Brad Delco, EVP of Finance & Chief Financial Officer, reported, "On a GAAP basis, revenue was down 2% year-over-year, while operating income improved 19% and diluted earnings per share improved 24% versus the prior year period." He noted, "In the fourth quarter, we executed over $25 million of track savings and are now on a run rate of over $100 million of annualized cost savings."
  • Delco highlighted capital allocation: "In 2025, we spent $575 million in capital reinvesting in our business... We put $923 million towards our share repurchasing, the largest annual amount in our company's history and retired almost 6.3 million shares of stock."
  • Spencer Frazier, EVP of Sales & Marketing, added, "Demand in the fourth quarter aligned with expectations, and we continue to see the truckload capacity bubble deflate... Last year, we had our highest customer retention since 2017."
  • Nicholas Hobbs, COO & President of Highway & Final Mile Services, stated, "2025 was our third consecutive year of record safety performance measured by DOT preventable accidents per million miles." He noted a $90 million revenue headwind in 2026 from anticipated legacy appliance-related business loss, but said, "We continue to work diligently to onboard new business in this area to offset as much of this as we can."
  • Bradley Hicks, President of Dedicated Contract Services, said, "We sold approximately 385 trucks of new deals, bringing our full year new truck sales to approximately 1,205 trucks." He expects "modest operating income growth in our dedicated business in 2026, with more momentum likely to roll into 2027."
  • Darren Field, EVP & President of Intermodal, shared, "Volumes in the quarter were down 2% year-over-year... Our focus remains on engaging in discussions and executing a strategy that is in the best interest of our customers and our shareholders."

Outlook

  • Simpson stated, "As we move into 2026, the freight market feels fragile. Capacity continues to exit the truckload market, and we are testing the elasticity of supply."
  • Delco projected net CapEx for 2026 to be in the range of "$600 million to $800 million, largely for replacement with expectations for success based growth capital to support our dedicated segment."
  • Hicks expects "only modest operating income growth in our dedicated business in 2026, with more momentum likely to roll into 2027."
  • Field commented on Intermodal: "We will live with the impact of this past bid season through the first half of 2026."

Financial Results

  • Delco reported, "On a GAAP basis, revenue was down 2% year-over-year, while operating income improved 19% and diluted earnings per share improved 24% versus the prior year period. In the prior year quarter, we did incur pretax charges of $16 million for intangible asset impairments. After consideration of these charges, operating income increased 10% from the prior year period."
  • For fiscal year 2025, "revenue declined 1% while operating income increased 4%."
  • Over $25 million in cost savings were executed in the fourth quarter, with a "run rate of over $100 million of annualized cost savings."
  • $923 million was spent on share repurchases, retiring 6.3 million shares.

Q&A

  • Brian Ossenbeck, JPMorgan: Asked about the fragility of the freight market. Simpson responded, "The elasticity in the supply chain from a supply perspective feels very fragile to us. There doesn't feel like a lot there... even small tightening is creating bigger ripples in the market than when it has historically."
  • Christian Wetherbee, Wells Fargo: Inquired about cost savings in 2026. Delco replied, "Going forward, I mean, I think it's fair to assume that we're going to be executing above the $100 million target. I don't think we're prepared right now to give you a number."
  • Jonathan Chappell, Evercore: Asked about peak season trends. Field said, "As we get into February and March, I think we'd have a better opportunity to understand what's going on there. But we're encouraged by what we've seen so far in January."
  • Scott Group, Wolfe Research: Questioned margin restoration. Field replied, "We have to sustain improvement in our margins. If we can utilize lower cost to serve to grow volume and continue to drive improvements from the volume front, we're going to be doing that."
  • Brady Lierz, Stephens: Asked about dedicated truck sales. Hicks highlighted, "We did have a record year in terms of new customer names... 40 new names also gives me a great promise for the work that we've done."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts expressed cautious optimism, frequently referencing market fragility and the need for clearer signs of sustainable demand. Questions focused on cost controls, margin outlook, and supply-demand balance, indicating a slightly positive but reserved tone.
  • Management maintained a tone of disciplined ambition, emphasizing operational excellence, cost control, and proactive growth, but acknowledged external uncertainties. Simpson stated, "We want to wait and see what happens. We want to finish up at least January and February, and see what happens from there."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, both analysts and management remained cautious, but current remarks reflected more optimism regarding cost savings and growth potential, while maintaining a conservative stance on demand recovery.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Management highlighted increased execution in cost savings, with $25 million in the fourth quarter compared to $20 million in the third quarter.
  • Operational excellence and safety performance continued to be emphasized as differentiators, with another year of record safety.
  • Strategic priorities shifted slightly toward leveraging prior investments and margin repair, while maintaining focus on disciplined growth.
  • Both quarters reflected concerns about market uncertainty and regulatory impacts, but this quarter featured more discussion of actionable growth initiatives and specific revenue headwinds in dedicated and final mile segments.
  • The tone remained cautious but with greater confidence in internal initiatives and cost control progress.
  • Analysts’ focus was consistent, seeking clarity on cost savings, margin trajectory, and market demand signals; management responses remained disciplined, with some increased willingness to discuss growth opportunities.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management flagged continued external market fragility and uncertain demand outlook.
  • A $90 million revenue headwind from legacy appliance business loss was explicitly identified.
  • Inflationary pressures, regulatory changes, and customer bankruptcies remain ongoing risks.
  • Management is focused on offsetting these risks through operational excellence, cost controls, and leveraging technology and prior investments.

Final Takeaway

J.B. Hunt’s leadership emphasized disciplined growth, operational excellence, and margin repair as it enters 2026 amid a fragile freight market. The company reported significant progress on cost savings initiatives, surpassing a $100 million annualized run rate, while maintaining strong service and safety standards. Management acknowledged persistent external uncertainties and specific revenue headwinds but highlighted a robust pipeline, high customer retention, and a strategy to leverage prior investments for future growth. The focus remains on creating shareholder value through disciplined capital allocation, proactive cost management, and maintaining a competitive advantage in service and technology, positioning the company for further momentum as market conditions evolve.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Crude oil slides by most since June as Iran risk premium fades
data.description:
Crude oil futures sank Thursday in their biggest drop since June after President Trump indicated the U.S. would hold off on attacking Iran for now, easing concerns over a military strike that could have threatened Iranian crude supply and key shipping routes.

The New York Times reported that Israel's Prime Minister Netanyahu and friendly Middle East governments asked Trump to postpone plans for a military attack on Iran, a day after Trump told reporters that he had been informed "the killing in Iran is stopping."

Analysts also noted a positive phone call between Trump and Venezuela's acting President Rodriguez, and Venezuela reportedly has begun reversing oil production cuts made under the U.S. embargo, with crude exports also resuming.

Also, the U.S. Energy Information Administration reported domestic crude and gasoline inventories rose last week by more than expected.

Rapid changes in the geopolitical situation are making 2026 tricky for oil markets as they flip between fundamental concerns and shifting risk premium, Neil Crosby of Sparta Commodities said in a note.

"Notional ideas rooted in fundamentals are going to frequently take a back seat in favor of the ever-increasing pace of change in global geopolitics," Crosby wrote. "While prices are in decline today on some relaxation in U.S. rhetoric, the next leg up may be in a matter of days."

Snapping five-session winning streaks. front-month Nymex crude (CL1:COM) for February delivery closed -4.5% to $59.19/bbl, and front-month Brent crude (CO1:COM) for March delivery ended -4.1% to $63.76/bbl, the largest one-day decline for both benchmarks since June 24.

The EIA reported natural gas inventories fell by 71 Bcf last week to 3,185 Bcf, which raised the surplus over the five-year average to 106 Bcf from 31 Bcf the week before; the front-month Nymex February natgas contract (NG1:COM) closed +0.2% to $3.128/MMBtu.

ETFs: (USO), (BNO), (UCO), (SCO), (USL), (DBO), (DRIP), (GUSH), (USOI), (UNG), (BOIL), (KOLD), (UNL), (FCG), (XLE)

data.title: Halliburton CEO sees speedy return to Venezuela, downplays risks - FT
data.description:
Halliburton (HAL) could be active within "months" in helping rebuild Venezuela's dilapidated oil and gas infrastructure, CEO Jeff Miller said Thursday in an interview with the Financial Times, playing down the risks of responding to President Trump's call for $100B in oil industry investment in the country.

Miller said the business model of companies in his sector meant they were not exposed to the same level of risk as oil and gas operators, which must sink large amounts of capital into projects over decades to earn a return.

"Obviously, we want stability and we have to be paid for our services," Miller told FT. "But the idea that we're putting assets in the ground that stay there 20 years [isn't the case]. Our assets move around, they can be moved out of countries, into countries. Very different profile to an oil and gas operator."

Trump has ruled out providing financial guarantees or assurances backed by U.S. taxpayer funds to oil companies that invest in Venezuela, but Miller said one possibility was money generated from the 50M barrels of oil Venezuela has begun turning over to U.S. authorities might be used to provide the oil industry with assurances.

Some analysts have said rival oilfield service company SLB (SLB) is the front-runner to win the first round of contracts in Venezuela because it retained an existing presence in the country despite U.S. sanctions, but Miller said Halliburton (HAL) could move equipment and skilled employees into Venezuela rapidly from other parts of Latin America and elsewhere.

"We can move quickly," Miller told FT. "We have 600 Venezuelans that work for Halliburton today all over the world. We’ve been there since 1938. I worked there. We have a number of our executives who have worked in Venezuela throughout their career. So we have the ability to move quickly."

data.title: Home BancShares outlines triple-accretive Tennessee entry and targets further M&A in 2026
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Home BancShares, Inc. (HOMB) Q4 2025

Management View

  • John Allison, Co-Founder, Chairman & CEO, opened by highlighting, “For the full year of 2025, the company earned a little over $475 million in net profit. That's an 18.2% increase over '24, and we ran a 2.05% ROA and a 41.29% efficiency ratio, had record revenue of $1.090 billion. We had earnings of $2.41 EPS. That's a 20% increase over 2024. We purchased for the year 2,890,706 shares for $81.3 million.”
  • Allison emphasized, “For the fourth quarter of '25, we reported $118 million in profit. That's 18% increase over the 2024...Net interest margin of 4.61%, and we built reserves to about 1.90%.”
  • He announced a significant move: “We're excited about our announced LOI with Mountain Commerce and an entry into the great state of Tennessee...our transaction is triple accretive and both sets of shareholders will be accreting the benefits of the merger on day 1, not some BS earn-back, but from day 1.”
  • Allison addressed broader industry challenges, stating, “We have allowed people to self-inflict the damage to ourselves and our industry, not just once, but over and over and over again by dilution, dilution, dilution.”
  • CEO Allison declared, “No more dilution from this point...The CEO of the bank should only make more money when he is responsible for increasing the EPS of the bank and make the shareholders a higher solid EPS increase.”
  • John Tipton, Chief Executive Officer, added, “The reported net interest margin improved to 4.61%, up 5 basis points from Q3 and up 22 basis points from the same period a year ago...Loan production was one of the highlights for the quarter at over $2.1 billion, highlighted by nearly $1.2 billion from the Community Bank footprint with half of that origination volume coming from Florida.”
  • Kevin Hester, President & Chief Lending Officer, noted, “When you combine the fourth quarter loan growth of $400 million with the loan growth that we posted through the first 3 quarters of the year, total loan growth for the year was $922 million or 6.24%.”
  • Christopher Poulton, President of Centennial Commercial Finance Group, reported, “We originated over $800 million in loan commitments, resulting in $236 million in net loan growth. This pulled outstanding loans into positive territory for the year with just under $200 million or 10% growth for the year.”
  • Brian Davis, CFO, Treasurer & Director, succinctly summarized, “It was a really good year. We blew it out and did quite a bit better than we even had budgeted.”

Outlook

  • Management expressed optimism for 2026, with Tipton stating, “We're excited to welcome the MCB employees, customers and shareholders to the Home family soon.”
  • Hester said, “While it will be tough to compete with 2025, we believe that 2026 will be equally as successful.”
  • Poulton projected, “I do expect paydowns to moderate growth in the near to midterm. But much like this past year, future funding and new volume may largely offset expected paydowns over the course of the year.”

Financial Results

  • Revenue for Q4 2025 was $282.1 million, with ROTCE of 16.65%.
  • Efficiency ratio for the quarter reached 39.53%, net interest margin was 4.61%, and reserves increased to 1.90%.
  • Noninterest-bearing balances comprised 22% of total deposits, which improved by over $150 million in Q4 and $334 million for the year.
  • Loan production for the quarter surpassed $2.1 billion, with Community Bank and CCFG both contributing strongly.
  • Expenses, excluding merger costs, were just shy of $114 million.
  • Share repurchases in Q4 totaled 540,706 shares for $14.7 million.

Q&A

  • Jon Arfstrom, RBC Capital Markets, asked about the drivers of loan growth. Hester responded, “The size and the geography of the loans is similar. It's the same. Most of our loans -- larger loans tend to be construction loans...Pipelines are strong. I think it was helpful, the last quarter that there wasn't a lot of rate movement.”
  • Stephen Scouten, Piper Sandler & Co., inquired about sector exposure and competitive pressures. Hester explained, “That particular loan is a loan we've had for some time...It's not a brand-new relationship.” Tipton clarified deposit growth and strategy, “Mid-90s is probably where we would target. We ended the quarter at 89%...I think our approach is we've long said we don't run CD ads. We run -- we advertise the company's strength.”
  • Matt Olney, Stephens Inc., asked about loan pricing and margin. Scott Walter described, “We're seeing some really silly stuff...I mean there is crazy stuff out there. I think it does slow down a little bit when you don't have rate drops.” Tipton said, “If you exclude event income, we were at 4.56% for the quarter. We actually ended December at 4.59%.”
  • David Rochester, Cantor Fitzgerald, focused on multifamily loan growth. Poulton replied, “A lot of what we do is we have a kind of a roster of clients who we've done business with for a long time...What we are seeing in multifamily is there's a particularly bad vintage of multifamily from like 2021-ish range, plus or minus months, where those -- that vintage hasn't done that well.”
  • Catherine Mealor, KBW, asked about the Mountain Commerce acquisition’s impact. Tipton stated, “I think from a funding standpoint, I think that's something we'll model over time...when you mark the balance sheet, our initial indications are little to no impact on where our net interest margin has run here over the last couple of quarters.”

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts’ tone was largely positive, with questions centering on growth, margin sustainability, and M&A appetite. Several congratulated management on a strong year.
  • Management’s sentiment was confident and assertive in both prepared remarks and Q&A, frequently referencing strong performance and discipline. Allison’s statements such as “No more dilution from this point” and repeated emphasis on shareholder value reflected strong conviction.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management’s tone was more assertive about M&A strategy and industry critique, while analysts maintained a similar constructive and inquisitive tone.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The current quarter featured a definitive push into Tennessee via the Mountain Commerce acquisition, compared to last quarter’s mention of a pending LOI.
  • Loan growth accelerated, with year-end totals and quarterly growth both higher than Q3 figures.
  • Efficiency improved with a sub-40% ratio, while net interest margin continued to expand.
  • Management’s confidence and candidness regarding industry practices and M&A was more pronounced this quarter.
  • Analysts’ focus shifted further towards the execution and integration of new acquisitions and the sustainability of loan and margin trends.

Risks and Concerns

  • Hester noted, “The fourth quarter loan growth number was higher than we anticipated because of $150 million in payoffs that did not occur as scheduled...the migration of these payoffs into 2026 may dampen early loan growth expectations.”
  • Ongoing credit issues were discussed, particularly regarding a DFW apartment loan and a Texas C&I credit, both of which remain under active management.
  • Management acknowledged elevated paydowns could temper loan growth in coming quarters.
  • Competition for loans remains intense, with aggressive pricing and terms highlighted by management as industry-wide concerns.

Final Takeaway

Management underscored Home BancShares’ record year, strong profitability, and disciplined approach to both organic growth and acquisitions, specifically pointing to the triple-accretive Mountain Commerce deal as a catalyst for further expansion in Tennessee. The company’s leadership remains committed to high reserve levels, efficiency, and shareholder value, while openly addressing credit risk and competitive pressures. Integration of the Mountain Commerce acquisition and ongoing vigilance in credit quality and market discipline are set to define the bank’s approach in 2026, with further M&A on the table if opportunities align with performance and shareholder interests.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Disney's Kennedy hands off 'Star Wars' leadership
data.description:
It's time for a new wave to pick up the lightsaber for Disney (DIS), as Kathleen Kennedy is stepping down from her longtime position as the sensei of Star Wars.

Kennedy is departing Lucasfilm, where she's held a leadership position for nearly 14 years. She joined the production company as co-chair with Star Wars creator/director George Lucas, and became its only leader shortly afterward when Disney (DIS) bought the company and Lucas exited.

Dave Filoni, chief creative officer and a Lucas protégé, will take over creative leadership of Lucasfilm, while President/General Manager Lynwen Brennan will act as co-president and lead the business side. They'll report to Disney Entertainment Co-Chairman Alan Bergman.

Both are longtime company veterans: Filoni joined in 2005, while Brennan joined via special-effects stalwart Industrial Light & Magic in 1999.

Kennedy plans to return to full-time producing, including finishing upcoming Lucasfilm projects The Mandalorian and Grogu and Star Wars: Starfighter.

Her tenure has been notable for a broad expansion of Star Wars media content, including a sequel trilogy to the original Star Wars trilogy that produced the all-time leader in domestic grosses (the seventh "episode," Star Wars: The Force Awakens, with $936.7M) as well as a move into live-action and animated TV series, including The Mandalorian, Obi-Wan Kenobi, Ahsoka, and The Clone Wars.
